goal: Implement AI_Basic (layer 1) — dismiss redundant moves, status on statused target, Safeguard blocking, healing at full HP
abstract_tasks: Implement base score initialization: all 4 moves start at 20; disabled/0-PP moves get score 80
Implement AI_Basic status-on-statused-target check: if target already has non-volatile status, add +5 penalty to pure-status move effects (Sleep, Toxic, Poison, Paralyze, Confuse, etc.)
Implement AI_Basic Safeguard check: if target is behind Safeguard, dismiss (AIDiscourageMove +10) all status-inflicting move effects

Implement AI_Redundant table: check each of the 29 listed redundant conditions (Light Screen already up, already seeded, already confused, Transform already active, etc.) and AIDiscourageMove (+10) when redundant
Implement move selection final step: decrement all scores round-robin until one reaches 0, re-increment for tie resolution, randomly pick among tied minimum-score moves

source_data.ai_behavior.md:
## AI_Basic (Layer 1)
Applied to almost all trainers. Prevents obviously stupid moves:
- Redundant moves: Calls AI_Redundant to check if a move would be useless
- Status on statused target: If the target already has a non-volatile status condition, dismiss pure status moves
- Safeguard blocking: If the target is behind Safeguard, dismiss status moves
Effect: Adds a large penalty (via AIDiscourageMove) to make the move very unlikely.
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### Layer 1: AI_Basic
- Dismiss status-only moves if the player already has a status condition
- Dismiss status moves if the player has Safeguard active
- Uses AI_Redundant to check if a move's effect is already in play

### Score System
- Each of the enemy's 4 moves starts with a base score of 20
- Disabled or 0-PP moves get score 80
- AIDiscourageMove = add 10 to score (essentially dismiss)

### AI Redundancy Checks (redundant.asm)
Checks if a move effect is already active/useless:
| Effect | Redundant When |
|--------|---------------|
| Light Screen | Already active on enemy side |
| Mist | Already has Mist substatus |
| Focus Energy | Already has Focus Energy |
| Confuse | Player already confused OR has Safeguard |
| Transform | Already transformed |
| Reflect | Already active on enemy side |
| Substitute | Already has Substitute |
| Leech Seed | Player already seeded |
| Disable | Player already disabled |
| Encore | Player already encored |
| Snore/Sleep Talk | Enemy NOT asleep (redundant) |
| Mean Look | Player already trapped |
| Nightmare | Player not asleep OR already has Nightmare |
| Spikes | Already have Spikes on player side |
| Foresight | Player already identified |
| Perish Song | Player already has Perish count |
| Sandstorm | Weather already Sandstorm |
| Attract | Can't attract OR player already attracted |
| Safeguard | Already active on enemy side |
| Rain Dance | Weather already Rain |
| Sunny Day | Weather already Sun |
| Dream Eater | Player NOT asleep |
| Swagger | Player already confused |
| Future Sight | BUG: checks SCREENS_UNUSED bit |
| Heal/Morning Sun/Synthesis/Moonlight | Enemy HP already full |
| Teleport | Always redundant (NPC trainers can't flee) |
source_data.ai_data_tables.md:
### AI_BASIC (Layer 1)
- Status-only effects discouraged if foe already has status: Sleep, Toxic, Poison, Paralyze effects get +5 penalty

unstructured_notes: AI_Basic is the foundational layer applied to virtually every trainer. The key is calling AI_Redundant for each move before checking status-on-status and Safeguard. The redundant.asm table has exactly 29 entries matching pokecrystal — the Future Sight one is a known bug (checks SCREENS_UNUSED bit instead of future sight pending count) and must be faithfully reproduced. The score floor is 1 (never 0 after the round-robin decrement unless it is the selected move). Wild Pokemon bypass all AI layers and pick completely at random — that short-circuit must go first in AIChooseMove.

goal: QA audit of sprints 79-80 — verify scoring system, AI_Basic correctly dismisses useless moves, tiebreak randomness
abstract_tasks: Verify base score initialization: all 4 moves start at 20, disabled/0-PP moves at 80, confirmed against pokecrystal scoring.asm
Verify all 29 AI_Redundant entries trigger correctly — especially the Future Sight bug (checks wrong bit) and Teleport always-redundant behavior
Test AI_Basic status-on-statused: run headless battle where target has BRN, confirm AI dismisses Thunder Wave, Toxic, Spore, Sing with +5/+10 penalty
Test Safeguard blocking: run headless battle where target has Safeguard active, confirm all status moves receive AIDiscourageMove (+10)
Verify tiebreak randomness: with two moves tied at score 1 after round-robin, confirm roughly 50/50 selection over 1000 trials
Verify wild Pokemon path: wild Pokemon must pick moves uniformly at random, no AI layer applied

source_data.ai_behavior.md:
## AI_Basic (Layer 1)
Applied to almost all trainers. Prevents obviously stupid moves:
- Redundant moves: Calls AI_Redundant
- Status on statused target: dismiss pure status moves
- Safeguard blocking: dismiss status moves

## AI Known Bugs Summary
1. Mean Look on self-toxic: Encourages Mean Look when AI's own Pokemon is badly poisoned
4. Future Sight stacking: Doesn't check if Future Sight is already pending
5. CheckTypeMatchup assumption: AI's type checking can return wrong results due to register corruption
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### AIChooseMove
1. Wild Pokemon: attack completely at random (return immediately)
2. Link battles: return (link opponent handles their own moves)
3. If locked in (Rollout, Outrage, etc.): return
4. Initialize all 4 scores to 20; disabled/no-PP moves get 80
5. Apply each enabled AI layer from TrainerClassAttributes
6. Decrement all scores round-robin until one reaches 0
7. Re-increment to resolve ties
8. Zero out all non-minimum scores
9. Randomly pick among tied minimum-score moves

unstructured_notes: QA sprint for the AI architecture foundation. The scoring round-robin is subtle: all scores are decremented by 1 in sequence until one hits 0. That move is selected unless another already-at-0 exists — then re-increment to find ties. The final random pick must be uniform. Check that no trainer with 0 AI layers assigned crashes (should just use all 4 moves randomly or fall through to some default). Confirm layer bit flags are correctly read from trainer class attributes.

goal: Implement AI_Setup (layer 2) and AI_Types (layer 3) — encourage setup turn 1, type effectiveness scoring, immune move penalty
abstract_tasks: Implement AI_Setup: on enemy Pokemon's first turn, 50% chance to greatly encourage (-2) stat-up moves; on player's first turn, 50% chance to greatly encourage (-2) stat-down moves; after turn 1, ~88% chance to greatly discourage (+2) all stat-modifying moves
Implement AI_Types type immunity dismissal: call CheckTypeMatchup for each damaging move; if result is immune (0 effectiveness), add AIDiscourageMove (+10)
Implement AI_Types super-effective encouragement: if type matchup result is super-effective, decrease score by 1 (-1)

Implement AI_Types not-very-effective discouragement: if type matchup is not-very-effective AND the enemy has at least one other damaging move of a different type, increase score by 1 (+1); if all damaging moves share the same type, skip this penalty
Track turn counters: enemy Pokemon first-turn flag and player Pokemon first-turn flag needed by AI_Setup

source_data.ai_behavior.md:
### AI_Setup (Layer 2)
Encourages stat-modifying moves on turn 1:
- Stat-up moves: 50% chance to strongly encourage (-2 to score) on FIRST turn of the enemy Pokemon
- Stat-down moves: 50% chance to strongly encourage on the first turn of the PLAYER's Pokemon
- After turn 1, ~88% chance to greatly discourage (+2 to score)

### AI_Types (Layer 3)
Considers type matchups:
- Immune moves: Heavily penalized (AIDiscourageMove)
- Super effective moves: Score decreased by 1
- Not very effective moves: Score increased by 1 IF the enemy has another move of a different type that deals damage
- Neutral moves: No change
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### Layer 2: AI_Setup
- 50% chance to greatly encourage stat-up moves on enemy's first turn
- 50% chance to greatly encourage stat-down moves on player's first turn
- ~88% chance to greatly discourage stat-modifying moves on later turns

### Layer 3: AI_Types
- Dismiss immune moves (add 10)
- Encourage super-effective damaging moves (-1)
- Discourage not-very-effective damaging moves (+1) ONLY if the enemy has at least one damaging move of a different type

### Probability Helpers
- AI_50_50: 50% chance (returns carry)
- AI_80_20: 80% chance (returns carry ~20% of the time, so 80% proceeds)
- BattleRandom: generates random byte 0-255

unstructured_notes: AI_Setup is designed to reward trainers for using setup moves on the opening turn and punish them for wasting setup on later turns. The ~88% discouragement figure comes from comparing BattleRandom against $E0 (224/256 ≈ 87.5%). For AI_Types, the 'different type damaging move' check requires iterating all 4 moves — if all four damaging moves are the same type, the not-very-effective penalty is skipped because the AI has no better type option. Note: AI_Types only modifies scores for damaging moves (base power > 0). Status moves are not scored by AI_Types.

goal: Implement AI_Offensive (layer 4) and AI_Smart (layer 5) — discourage status moves, context-specific scoring (the complex one)
abstract_tasks: Implement AI_Offensive: greatly discourage (+2) any move with base power 0 (status-only moves)
Implement AI_Smart dispatch table: 80+ effect-specific handlers; build the dispatch map keyed on move effect ID constants

Implement the high-priority AI_Smart handlers: Sleep moves, Leech Hit, Selfdestruct/Explosion (HP threshold logic), Dream Eater, Evasion Up, Paralyze, Toxic, Protect, Perish Song, Priority Hit, Counter, Mirror Coat, Encore, Heal/Recovery, Baton Pass, Rollout, Fury Cutter, Sandstorm, Substitute, Hyper Beam, Endure, Heal Bell, Mean Look bug (checks own toxic), Conversion2 bug (backwards condition)

Implement AI_Smart weather synergy handlers: Rain Dance (encourages with Water/Thunder moves; discourages if player is Water), Sunny Day (encourages with Fire/recovery moves but NOT SolarBeam/Flame Wheel/Moonlight per known bug), Solarbeam (sun encourages, rain discourages), Thunder in sun (discourage)

Implement remaining AI_Smart handlers: Disable, Nightmare, Curse ghost/non-ghost logic, Safeguard, Magnitude/Earthquake vs Dig, Pursuit, Rapid Spin, Hidden Power type/power calc, Attract, Swagger, Future Sight, Stomp vs Minimize, Snore/Sleep Talk, Spite, Bide, Force Switch, Reset Stats/Haze, Mirror Move, Mimic, Pain Split, Destiny Bond/Reversal, Flame Wheel vs Frozen

source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### Layer 4: AI_Offensive
- Greatly discourage (+2) any move with 0 base power

### Layer 5: AI_Smart
Purpose: Context-specific per-effect scoring. The most complex layer with 80+ effect handlers.

### Sleep Moves (EFFECT_SLEEP)
- If enemy has Dream Eater or Nightmare: 50% chance to greatly encourage (-2)
- Otherwise: 50% chance to greatly encourage (-2)

### Selfdestruct/Explosion (EFFECT_SELFDESTRUCT)
- If enemy's last mon AND player's last mon: don't discourage
- If enemy's last mon AND player has others: greatly discourage (+3)
- If enemy HP > 50%: greatly discourage (+3)
- If enemy HP < 25%: do nothing
- If enemy HP 25-50%: ~92% chance to greatly discourage (+3)

### Counter (EFFECT_COUNTER)
- Count player's known physical damaging moves
- 3+ physical moves: ~60% chance to encourage (-1)
- Last player move was physical damaging: ~60% chance to encourage (-1)
- No physical moves: discourage (+1)

### Mirror Coat (EFFECT_MIRROR_COAT)
- Same as Counter but checks for special (type >= SPECIAL) moves

### Encore (EFFECT_ENCORE)
- Enemy slower: greatly discourage (+3)
- Player's last move is in EncoreMoves list: ~72% chance to greatly encourage (-2)
- Otherwise: greatly discourage (+3)

### Mean Look (EFFECT_MEAN_LOOK)
- Enemy HP < 50%: discourage (+1)
- Player is last mon: dismiss (+10)
- BUG: Checks enemy's own toxic status instead of player's

### Conversion2 (EFFECT_CONVERSION2)
- BUG: Checks wLastPlayerMove but condition discourages WHEN player HAS used a move (backwards)

### Sunny Day (EFFECT_SUNNY_DAY)
- BUG: Does NOT encourage Sunny Day with SolarBeam, Flame Wheel, or Moonlight

### Rain Dance / Encourage list:
Water Gun, Hydro Pump, Surf, BubbleBeam, Thunder, Waterfall, Clamp, Bubble, Crabhammer, Octazooka, Whirlpool

### Sunny Day / Encourage list:
Fire Punch, Ember, Flamethrower, Fire Spin, Fire Blast, Sacred Fire, Morning Sun, Synthesis

### Encore encourage list (EncoreMoves):
Swords Dance, Whirlwind, Leer, Roar, Disable, Mist, Leech Seed, Growth, PoisonPowder, String Shot, Meditate, Agility, Teleport, Screech, Haze, Focus Energy, Dream Eater, Poison Gas, Splash, Sharpen, Conversion, Super Fang, Substitute, Triple Kick, Spider Web, Mind Reader, Flame Wheel, Aeroblast, Cotton Spore, Powder Snow

unstructured_notes: AI_Smart is the largest layer with ~80 effect handlers. Two known bugs must be faithfully reproduced: (1) Mean Look checks the AI's own toxic status flag instead of the player's, causing it to encourage Mean Look when the AI's own Pokemon is poisoned. (2) Conversion2 condition is backwards — it discourages when the player HAS used a move rather than when they haven't. Both are documented in ai_behavior.md bugs summary. The Sunny Day synergy bug (missing SolarBeam/Flame Wheel/Moonlight) must also be reproduced. The Rain Dance encourage list and Sunny Day encourage list are hardcoded move-ID tables in the ASM — implement them as static lookup tables.

goal: QA audit of sprints 82-83 — verify AI layer interactions, Smart layer context decisions, type effectiveness calculations
abstract_tasks: Verify AI_Setup turn-1 behavior: in headless battles, confirm stat-up moves are encouraged on turn 1 (about 50% of time) and heavily discouraged on turn 2+ (~88% rate)
Verify AI_Types immune dismissal: confirm moves that hit immune targets always receive +10 (dismiss) penalty; test Ghost vs Normal, Electric vs Ground, etc.

Verify AI_Types NVE penalty conditional: set up a trainer with one NVE move and no other type coverage — confirm NVE penalty is NOT applied; then add a second move of different type — confirm penalty IS applied
Verify AI_Smart Selfdestruct HP logic: at >50% HP discourage (+3); at <25% no adjustment; confirm the AI rarely self-destructs when healthy

Verify AI_Smart known bugs: (1) Mean Look on self-toxic fires when AI's own Pokemon is poisoned, not player; (2) Conversion2 discourages when player HAS moved; (3) Sunny Day does not get encouraged by SolarBeam in move list
Verify AI_Offensive: all 0-base-power moves receive +2 on every battle turn for any trainer using layer 4

## AI Known Bugs Summary
1. Mean Look on self-toxic: Encourages Mean Look when the AI's own Pokemon is badly poisoned
2. Conversion2 after turn 1: Discourages Conversion2 after the first turn for no good reason
3. Sun-synergy blindness: Doesn't recognize SolarBeam/Flame Wheel/Moonlight benefits from Sunny Day
4. Future Sight stacking: Doesn't check if Future Sight is already pending
5. CheckTypeMatchup assumption: AI's type checking can return wrong results due to register corruption

### AI_Types (Layer 3)
- Immune moves: Heavily penalized (AIDiscourageMove)
- Super effective moves: Score decreased by 1
- Not very effective moves: Score increased by 1 IF enemy has another move of a different type that deals damage
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### Layer 2: AI_Setup
- 50% chance to greatly encourage stat-up moves on enemy's first turn
- ~88% chance to greatly discourage stat-modifying moves on later turns

### Layer 4: AI_Offensive
- Greatly discourage (+2) any move with 0 base power

unstructured_notes: This QA sprint validates the first three non-trivial AI layers plus AI_Smart's most complex behaviors. The key testing challenge is the probabilistic nature: use many trials (1000+) to verify approximate percentages. The known bugs should have dedicated tests confirming they fire under the exact documented conditions. Also test layer interaction: a trainer using layers 1+3+5 should correctly stack all three score modifications on each move.

goal: Implement AI_Cautious (layer 6), AI_Status (layer 7), AI_Risky (layer 8) — risk-averse, status-encouraging, high-risk behaviors
abstract_tasks: Implement AI_Opportunist (pokecrystal layer 6 — named AI_Cautious in master): if HP < 25%, discourage stall moves (+1); if HP 25-50%, 50% chance to discourage stall moves (+1); use the hardcoded stall moves list from data/battle/ai/stall_moves.asm

Implement AI_Aggressive (pokecrystal layer 7): calculate actual damage with AIDamageCalc for each damaging move; discourage (+1) all damaging moves except the highest-damage one; exempt moves with power 0-1 (Seismic Toss, Counter, etc.) and reckless moves (Selfdestruct, rampage, multi-hit) from comparison

Implement AI_Cautious (pokecrystal layer 8): 90% chance to discourage (+1) residual-effect moves after the first turn; use hardcoded residual moves list from data/battle/ai/residual_moves.asm; reproduce the known bug where ret nc should be jr nc causing early return without checking all moves
Implement AI_Status (pokecrystal layer 9): dismiss Toxic/Poison if target is Poison-type; dismiss any status or damaging move with type immunity (0 effectiveness)

Implement AI_Risky (pokecrystal layer 10): calculate damage for each damaging move; if damage >= player's remaining HP, maximally encourage (-5); for risky moves (Selfdestruct effects) at max HP, skip or 80% chance to skip

source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### Layer 6: AI_Opportunist
- If HP < 25%: discourage stall moves (+1)
- If HP 25-50%: 50% chance to discourage stall moves (+1)
- Stall moves defined in data/battle/ai/stall_moves.asm

### Layer 7: AI_Aggressive
- Calculates actual damage for each damaging move (using AIDamageCalc)
- Discourages (+1) all damaging moves except the one that does the most damage
- Ignores moves with power 0-1 (Seismic Toss, Counter, etc.)
- Ignores reckless moves (Selfdestruct, etc.) from the comparison

### Layer 8: AI_Cautious
- 90% chance to discourage (+1) residual-effect moves after the first turn
- Residual moves defined in data/battle/ai/residual_moves.asm
- BUG: The ret nc after the random check should be jr nc, .loop, causing early return without checking all moves

### Layer 9: AI_Status
- Dismiss Toxic/Poison against Poison-type targets
- Dismiss any status or damaging move with 0 type matchup (immune)

### Layer 10: AI_Risky
- If damage >= player's remaining HP: maximally encourage (-5)
- Risky moves (Self-Destruct effects) at max HP: skip or 80% chance to skip

### Score Modification Conventions
- dec [hl] x5 = maximally encourage (used by AI_Risky for KO moves)
source_data.ai_data_tables.md:
### AI_CAUTIOUS (Layer 2)
- Residual moves discouraged after first turn:
  - Mist, Leech Seed, PoisonPowder, Stun Spore, Thunder Wave
  - Focus Energy, Bide, Poison Gas, Transform, Conversion, Substitute, Spikes

### AI_OPPORTUNIST (Layer 4)
- Stall moves discouraged when HP is low:
  - Swords Dance, Tail Whip, Leer, Growl, Disable, Mist, Counter, Leech Seed
  - Growth, String Shot, Meditate, Agility, Rage, Mimic, Screech, Harden
  - Withdraw, Defense Curl, Barrier, Light Screen, Haze, Reflect
  - Focus Energy, Bide, Amnesia, Transform, Splash, Acid Armor
  - Sharpen, Conversion, Substitute, Flame Wheel

### AI_RISKY (Layer 5)
- Will not use risky moves at max HP even if they KO:
  - Self-Destruct/Explosion effects
  - OHKO moves (Fissure, Guillotine, Horn Drill)

unstructured_notes: Note: the layer numbering between ai_behavior.md and ai_scoring_details.md differs — ai_scoring_details.md numbers them 1-10 mapping to constants, while the master sprint sequence uses descriptive names AI_Cautious/AI_Status/AI_Risky for layers 6-8. Cross-reference carefully: AI_Opportunist (scoring layer 6) = AI_Cautious behavior; AI_Aggressive (layer 7) = AI_Status name in behavior doc; AI_Cautious (layer 8) = residual discouragement. AIDamageCalc in AI_Aggressive and AI_Risky must use the same damage formula as actual battles but with approximate stat values from trainer party data. The layer 8 ret-nc bug is specifically that after a failed 90% random check, the function returns from the entire layer instead of skipping just that move.

goal: Implement AI switching logic — when to switch, switch scoring, HP threshold, type disadvantage detection
abstract_tasks: Implement CheckAbleToSwitch: (1) verify alive party members exist to switch to; (2) if Perish Song counter == 1, always attempt switch; (3) if player has shown a super-effective move against current Pokemon, attempt switch

Implement switch candidate filtering pipeline: FindAliveEnemyMons -> FindEnemyMonsWithAtLeastQuarterMaxHP (> 25% HP filter) -> FindEnemyMonsThatResistPlayer (resist player's shown moves) -> FindAliveEnemyMonsWithASuperEffectiveMove (has SE move vs player)

Implement switch frequency table: read trainer attribute SwitchOften/SwitchSometimes/SwitchRarely; at Low Priority score ($10): 50%/20%/8%; at Med Priority ($20): 79%/50%/12%; at High Priority ($30): 96%/80%/79%
Implement trap check: if current Pokemon is under Mean Look or Spider Web, skip switch logic entirely (can't switch)

Implement gym leader / E4 switch profile: they use Full Restore usage at low HP and are more likely to switch than regular trainers; confirm their trainer class attributes include SwitchSometimes or SwitchOften

source_data.ai_behavior.md:
## AI Switching (engine/battle/ai/switch.asm)
The AI considers switching when:
- Its Pokemon is at a significant type disadvantage
- Its Pokemon has been badly statused
- A party member has a much better type matchup

Switch decision factors:
- Type effectiveness of the player's last move against current Pokemon
- HP remaining on the current Pokemon
- Whether a party member resists/is immune to the player's moves
- Whether the current Pokemon is trapped (Mean Look, etc.)

In practice, most regular trainers rarely switch. Gym Leaders and E4 members with AI_SMART are more likely to switch strategically.
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
## AI Switch Logic (switch.asm)

### CheckAbleToSwitch
1. Whether the enemy has alive mons to switch to
2. Perish Song at count 1: always try to switch
3. Player has super-effective moves: try to switch
4. Search for a mon that: has > 25% HP, resists the player, has a super-effective move

### Switch Frequency (from trainer attributes)
| Attribute | Low Priority (score $10) | Med Priority ($20) | High Priority ($30) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| SwitchOften | 50% switch | 79% switch | 96% switch |
| SwitchSometimes | 20% switch | 50% switch | 80% switch |
| SwitchRarely | 8% switch | 12% switch | 79% switch |

### Switch Candidate Selection
1. FindAliveEnemyMons -- find all non-fainted mons
2. FindEnemyMonsWithAtLeastQuarterMaxHP -- filter to > 25% HP
3. FindEnemyMonsThatResistPlayer -- filter to those resisting player's shown moves
4. FindAliveEnemyMonsWithASuperEffectiveMove -- further filter to those with SE moves

unstructured_notes: The switch score ($10/$20/$30 priority tiers) comes from how urgently the situation demands a switch: $10 is marginal (player has SE move), $20 is moderate (both SE move and type disadvantage), $30 is critical (Perish Song count 1 or severe type disadvantage). The switch candidate selection is a cascading filter — if no candidate passes all four filters, the AI does not switch. The trap check (Mean Look, Wrap, Spider Web, etc.) blocks switching entirely for the trapped Pokemon. Regular trainers without SwitchOften or SwitchSometimes effectively never switch in practice.

goal: QA audit of sprints 85-86 — verify all 8 AI layers, switching decisions, layer combinations per trainer class
abstract_tasks: Verify AI_Opportunist stall-move discouragement: at <25% HP, confirm stall move list items receive +1 every turn; at 50-75% HP, confirm ~50% rate of discouragement

Verify AI_Aggressive best-move selection: with a trainer having Flamethrower + Tackle + Growl + Thunderbolt, confirm Flamethrower or Thunderbolt (highest damage vs specific target) is selected most often
Verify AI_Cautious residual-move bug: confirm that after the 90% check fails on one move, the remaining moves in the list are NOT penalized in that call (early return bug must be present)
Verify AI_Risky KO encouragement: set up scenario where one move KOs the player; confirm that move receives -5 (maximally encouraged) and is selected overwhelmingly
Verify switch logic: run 100 battles with a trainer using SwitchOften vs a SE matchup; confirm switching rate is approximately 50% at low priority; confirm trapped Pokemon never switches
Verify complete layer combinations: Falkner (Basic+Types+Smart), Whitney (Basic+Types+Smart+Setup), Morty (Basic+Types+Smart) — confirm each trainer class applies the correct layer bitmask

source_data.ai_behavior.md:
### Gym Leaders (Johto)
- Falkner: Basic AI, straightforward attacking
- Whitney: Known for Miltank's Rollout + Milk Drink combo
- Morty: Uses Mean Look + Curse/Hypnosis combo

### Elite Four
- They use Full Restores at low HP
- They employ type-coverage strategies
- They switch more frequently than regular trainers
- AI_SMART layer handles most of their decision making
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
### Layer Application
- Trainer class attributes are stored as a 16-bit flag field
- Each bit enables one AI layer (layers 1-16)
- Battle Tower always uses the first trainer class's attributes (Falkner's)

### Layer 8: AI_Cautious BUG
- The ret nc after the random check should be jr nc, .loop, causing the function to sometimes return early without checking all moves

### Layer 10: AI_Risky
- If damage >= player's remaining HP: maximally encourage (-5)

unstructured_notes: The Battle Tower note is important for testing: all BT trainers use Falkner's AI layer bitmask, which is layers 1+3+5 (Basic, Types, Smart). This should be verifiable via headless BT runs. Also verify that the layer bitmask is a 16-bit field stored in trainer class attributes — not per-trainer — meaning all trainers of the same class share AI layers. Test the Morty Mean Look bug: set AI's own Pokemon to badly-poisoned and confirm Mean Look score is encouraged even when player is not poisoned.

goal: Implement AI item usage — trainer item slots, potion thresholds, Full Restore logic, status healing prioritization
abstract_tasks: Implement AI_TryItem: read wEnemyTrainerItem1 and wEnemyTrainerItem2 from trainer data; only use items on the highest-level party member; look up item ID in AI_Items dispatch table

Implement healing item conditions: Potion at low HP threshold (varies by item), Super Potion at moderate threshold, Hyper Potion at higher threshold, Max Potion / Full Restore at critical HP; Full Restore also cures status on use
Implement status item condition: Full Heal used when current Pokemon has any non-volatile status; reproduce known bug — Full Heal does NOT cure Nightmare, confusion, or stat drops from burn/paralysis

Implement stat item usage: X Attack, X Defend, X Speed, X Special, X Accuracy, Dire Hit used based on AI layer context; Guard Spec prevents stat drops; items cost the AI's turn (no attacking on item-use turn)

Implement item consumption: each trainer has a fixed item set (up to 2 items); items are consumed after use and cannot be used again; reproduce known bug — base reward money can be accidentally read as item index

source_data.ai_behavior.md:
## AI Item Usage (engine/battle/ai/items.asm)
Trainers can be configured to use items. The AI checks conditions before using each item type:

### Healing Items
- Potion / Super Potion / Hyper Potion / Max Potion / Full Restore: Used when HP drops below a threshold
- Full Restore also heals status conditions

### Status Items
- Full Heal: Used when the Pokemon has a non-volatile status condition
- BUG: Doesn't cure Nightmare or confusion
- BUG: Doesn't restore Attack/Speed drops from burn/paralysis

### Stat Items
- X Attack, X Defend, X Speed, X Special, X Accuracy, Dire Hit: Used strategically
- Guard Spec: Prevents stat drops

### Usage Limits
- Each trainer has a fixed set of items they can use
- Items are consumed when used (can't be used again)
- The AI uses items INSTEAD of attacking (costs the turn)
- BUG: The AI might try to use its base reward money as an item index
source_data.ai_scoring_details.md:
## AI Item Usage (items.asm)

### AI_TryItem
- Not used in Battle Tower (items banned)
- Checks wEnemyTrainerItem1 and wEnemyTrainerItem2
- Only uses items on the highest-level party member
- Looks up item in AI_Items table
- Each item has a condition check and use routine
- BUG: Base reward value can accidentally be read as an item due to pointer math

### AI Item Categories
Trainers carry up to 2 items, used based on HP thresholds and status conditions:
- Full Heal: used when current mon is statused
- Full Restore: used when HP is critical (varies by trainer)
- Potions: used at various HP thresholds
- X Items: used to boost stats

unstructured_notes: The 'only use items on the highest-level party member' rule is quirky — it means the AI only heals or boosts its ace Pokemon, not a weakened lower-level slot member. This effectively buffers the AI's strongest Pokemon at the cost of fairness. Battle Tower explicitly disables items (items banned), so AI_TryItem must check if it's a BT battle and skip immediately. The reward-money-as-item bug is a pointer arithmetic error in the ASM — the fix would be a bounds check, but pokecrystal does not have it, so we must not add one.

goal: Implement AI data tables — stat multiplier tables, critical hit chance tables, weather modifier tables, type matchup caching
abstract_tasks: Implement critical hit stage table: stage 0=1/16, stage 1=1/8, stage 2=1/4, stage 3=1/3, stage 4+=1/2 (capped); implement crit stage modifiers: high-crit moves +1, Focus Energy +1, Scope Lens +1, Lucky Punch +2, Stick +2, Dire Hit +1

Implement weather type modifier table: Rain boosts Water 1.5x and weakens Fire 0.5x; Sun boosts Fire 1.5x and weakens Water 0.5x; Rain weakens SolarBeam 0.5x; confirm Sandstorm has no damage modifier (only chip damage)

Implement held item battle effects tables: consumable items (berry auto-heal, PSNCureBerry, Burnt Berry, Ice Berry, Mint Berry, PRZCureBerry, MiracleBerry, Smoke Ball); stat-up items table for HELD_ATTACK_UP through HELD_EVASION_UP; status heal table mapping HELD_HEAL_* to status cured

## Stat Modifier Multipliers
| Stage | Multiplier | Percentage |
|-------|-----------|------------|
| -6 | 25/100 | 25% |
| -5 | 28/100 | 28% |
| -4 | 33/100 | 33% |
| -3 | 40/100 | 40% |
| -2 | 50/100 | 50% |
| -1 | 66/100 | 66% |
| 0 | 1/1 | 100% |
| +1 | 15/10 | 150% |
| +2 | 2/1 | 200% |
| +3 | 25/10 | 250% |
| +4 | 3/1 | 300% |
| +5 | 35/10 | 350% |
| +6 | 4/1 | 400% |

## Accuracy Modifier Multipliers
| -6 | 33/100 | -5 | 36/100 | -4 | 43/100 | -3 | 50/100 | -2 | 60/100 | -1 | 75/100 | 0 | 1/1 | +1 | 133/100 | +2 | 166/100 | +3 | 2/1 | +4 | 233/100 | +5 | 133/50 | +6 | 3/1 |

## Critical Hit Chances
| Crit Stage | Chance |
|-----------|--------|
| +0 | 1/16 (6.25%) |
| +1 | 1/8 (12.5%) |
| +2 | 1/4 (25%) |
| +3 | 1/3 (33.3%) |
| +4 | 1/2 (50%) |
| +5 | 1/2 (50%, capped) |

Crit stage modifiers: high-crit moves +1, Focus Energy +1, Scope Lens +1, Lucky Punch (Chansey) +2, Stick (Farfetch'd) +2, Dire Hit +1

## Weather Modifiers
| Weather | Type | Effect |
|---------|------|--------|
| Rain | Water | 1.5x damage |
| Rain | Fire | 0.5x damage |
| Sun | Fire | 1.5x damage |
| Sun | Water | 0.5x damage |
| Rain | SolarBeam | 0.5x damage |

## Held Item Battle Effects
### Consumable Effects
- HELD_BERRY (Berry, Gold Berry) — Auto-heal HP
- HELD_HEAL_POISON (PSNCureBerry) — Auto-cure poison
- HELD_HEAL_FREEZE (Burnt Berry) — Auto-cure freeze
- HELD_HEAL_BURN (Ice Berry) — Auto-cure burn
- HELD_HEAL_SLEEP (Mint Berry) — Auto-cure sleep
- HELD_HEAL_PARALYZE (PRZCureBerry) — Auto-cure paralysis
- HELD_HEAL_STATUS (MiracleBerry) — Auto-cure any status
- HELD_ESCAPE (Smoke Ball) — Guaranteed escape

### Stat-Up Items (held_stat_up.asm)
| Held Effect | Stat |
|------------|------|
| HELD_ATTACK_UP | Attack |
| HELD_DEFENSE_UP | Defense |
| HELD_SPEED_UP | Speed |
| HELD_SP_ATTACK_UP | Sp. Attack |
| HELD_SP_DEFENSE_UP | Sp. Defense |
| HELD_ACCURACY_UP | Accuracy |
| HELD_EVASION_UP | Evasion |

unstructured_notes: These data tables are the ground truth for all stat and accuracy calculations during battle. The accuracy multiplier table is deliberately different from the stat multiplier table — they do NOT share the same values. The critical hit cap at stage 4+ = 1/2 means Lucky Punch Chansey (base +2) with Focus Energy (+1) and Dire Hit (+1) reaches stage 4 and caps at 50%. The weather modifier system uses the same MORE_EFFECTIVE / NOT_VERY_EFFECTIVE encoding as the type chart — it stacks multiplicatively with type effectiveness.

goal: QA audit of sprints 88-89 — verify AI item usage timing, data table accuracy, full AI integration across all layers
abstract_tasks: Verify AI item usage timing: confirm item use replaces the attack action (only one action per turn); confirm Battle Tower correctly blocks all item usage
Verify potion HP thresholds: set up trainer with Hyper Potion, run battles where HP drops to various levels; confirm item triggers only below threshold
Verify Full Heal bug: set Pokemon to BRN status, use Full Heal — status should be cured; then set to Nightmare — confirm Full Heal does NOT cure it (faithfully reproduce bug)
Verify stat/accuracy multiplier tables: compute Lv50 Alakazam Sp.Atk at +6 stage (base 135 * 4/1 = 540, cap at 999); verify accuracy at -6 stage is 33% of base
Verify critical hit chance: Slash (high-crit +1) at stage 1 = 1/8; Farfetch'd with Stick (+2) using Slash (+1) = stage 3 = 1/3; add Scope Lens (+1) = stage 4 = 1/2 capped
Verify full AI integration: run 200-battle headless simulation with Lance's Dragonite (layers Basic+Types+Smart); confirm DragonBreath usage is not excessively suppressed by NVE on Dragon types

unstructured_notes: This is the final QA for the entire AI system before moving to overworld. The integration test with Lance is meaningful — his Dragonite knows DragonBreath (Dragon-type, SE vs Dragon), Hyper Beam (Normal, neutral), Blizzard (Ice), and Thunder (Electric). AI_Types should encourage DragonBreath vs Dragon targets. AI_Smart should discourage Hyper Beam unless HP is low. Run enough battles to see Full Restore usage trigger and verify it also cures status (distinguishing Full Restore from Hyper Potion in testing). After this QA passes, the entire AI Phase (sprints 79-90) is complete.

goal: Implement overworld main loop — 3-state map machine (START/ENTER/HANDLE), per-frame processing, player event priority chain
abstract_tasks: Implement OverworldLoop 3-state machine driven by wMapStatus: MAPSTATUS_START (clear state, init phone delay, run map setup), MAPSTATUS_ENTER (run map setup script, set 5-step wild encounter cooldown), MAPSTATUS_HANDLE (main per-frame processing)

Implement MAPSTATUS_HANDLE per-frame sequence: ResetOverworldDelay (2 frames max), HandleMapTimeAndJoypad (UpdateTime + GetJoypad + TimeOfDayPals), HandleCmdQueue, MapEvents (PlayerEvents + ScriptEvents), HandleMapObjects (NPC step + player step + object visibility), NextOverworldFrame, HandleMapBackground (sprites + scrolling + map name sign), CheckPlayerState

Implement PlayerEvents priority chain (first match wins): (1) CheckTrainerEvent, (2) CheckTileEvent (warps, connections, coord events, step counting, wild encounters), (3) RunMemScript, (4) RunSceneScript, (5) CheckTimeEvents (Bug Contest timer, daily reset, Pokerus tick, phone calls), (6) OWPlayerInput (A-button interact, Start menu, Select registered item)

Implement wEnabledPlayerEvents bit flags: bit 0 WARPS_AND_CONNECTIONS, bit 1 COORD_EVENTS, bit 2 COUNT_STEPS, bit 3 WILD_ENCOUNTERS, bit 5 UNUSED; CheckPlayerState enables/disables flags based on step state
Implement player event type constants (PLAYEREVENT_*): NONE=0, SEENBYTRAINER=1, TALKTOTRAINER=2, ITEMBALL=3, CONNECTION=4, WARP=5, FALL=6, WHITEOUT=7, HATCH=8, JOYCHANGEFACING=9

source_data.overworld_engine.md:
## Main Loop (events.asm)

### OverworldLoop
Three-state machine driven by wMapStatus:
1. MAPSTATUS_START (StartMap) -- Clear state, init phone delay, run map setup
2. MAPSTATUS_ENTER (EnterMap) -- Run map setup script, set 5-step wild encounter cooldown
3. MAPSTATUS_HANDLE (HandleMap) -- Main per-frame processing:
   - ResetOverworldDelay (2 frames max)
   - HandleMapTimeAndJoypad (UpdateTime, GetJoypad, TimeOfDayPals)
   - HandleCmdQueue (queued commands)
   - MapEvents (PlayerEvents + ScriptEvents)
   - HandleMapObjects (NPC step + player step + object visibility)
   - NextOverworldFrame (delay remaining)
   - HandleMapBackground (sprites, scrolling, map name sign)
   - CheckPlayerState (enable/disable events based on step flags)

### Player Event Priority (PlayerEvents)
Checked in order; first match wins:
1. CheckTrainerEvent -- Trainer line of sight
2. CheckTileEvent -- Warps, connections, coord events, step counting, wild encounters
3. RunMemScript -- Queued reentry scripts
4. RunSceneScript -- Scene-based scripts (wCurMapSceneScriptCount)
5. CheckTimeEvents -- Bug Contest timer, daily reset, Pokerus tick, phone calls
6. OWPlayerInput -- A-button (talk/interact), Start (menu), Select (registered item)

### Enabled Player Events (wEnabledPlayerEvents)
Bit flags controlling which events are active:
- Bit 0: PLAYEREVENTS_WARPS_AND_CONNECTIONS
- Bit 1: PLAYEREVENTS_COORD_EVENTS
- Bit 2: PLAYEREVENTS_COUNT_STEPS
- Bit 3: PLAYEREVENTS_WILD_ENCOUNTERS
- Bit 5: PLAYEREVENTS_UNUSED

### Player Event Types (PLAYEREVENT_*)
| 0 | PLAYEREVENT_NONE | No event |
| 1 | PLAYEREVENT_SEENBYTRAINER | Spotted by trainer |
| 2 | PLAYEREVENT_TALKTOTRAINER | Talked to trainer |
| 3 | PLAYEREVENT_ITEMBALL | Found item ball |
| 4 | PLAYEREVENT_CONNECTION | Map connection |
| 5 | PLAYEREVENT_WARP | Warp tile |
| 6 | PLAYEREVENT_FALL | Pit/fall hole |
| 7 | PLAYEREVENT_WHITEOUT | White out |
| 8 | PLAYEREVENT_HATCH | Egg hatching |
| 9 | PLAYEREVENT_JOYCHANGEFACING | Direction change |

unstructured_notes: This sprint begins Phase 6: Overworld Engine. The 3-state map machine is the outermost architectural structure of the entire overworld — everything else is called from MAPSTATUS_HANDLE. The priority chain order is critical: trainer line-of-sight firing before tile events means a trainer can intercept the player before a warp fires. The 5-step wild encounter cooldown set on MAPSTATUS_ENTER prevents instant encounters when entering a grass area. wEnabledPlayerEvents gives fine-grained control over what can trigger — used to lock warps during cutscenes, disable wild encounters during scripted events, etc.

goal: Implement player movement — joypad input, 4-direction walking, running shoes equivalent, step counting, collision checking
abstract_tasks: Implement DoPlayerMovement: GetDPad reads joypad and applies downhill forcing; TranslateIntoMovement dispatches based on player state (PLAYER_NORMAL, PLAYER_BIKE, PLAYER_SURF, PLAYER_SURF_PIKA, PLAYER_SKATE)

Implement normal/bike movement pipeline: CheckForced (ice locks direction) -> GetAction (maps D-pad to walking direction, facing, deltaX/Y, collision tile) -> CheckTile (forced movement tiles override input) -> CheckTurning (turn in place if facing != walking direction) -> TryStep (land permissions + NPC collision + ice + bike speed) -> TryJump (ledge hopping on tiles $A0-$A7) -> CheckWarp (edge warp detection)
Implement movement step types and speeds: STEP_WALK (normal slow_step), STEP_BIKE (fast big_step), STEP_ICE (fast_slide_step), STEP_LEDGE (jump_step), STEP_TURN (turn in place)

Implement collision checks: CheckLandPerms (tile permissions + walkability), CheckSurfPerms (0=water, 1=land exit, carry=blocked), CheckNPC (scan all 16 object structs for collision at target coordinates), CheckStrengthBoulder (if Strength active and STRENGTH_BOULDER_F set, trigger BOULDER_MOVING_F)

Implement ice sliding: PLAYER_SKATE state locks direction input (CheckForced); movement continues in same direction until hitting WALL_TILE or COLL_BRAKE tile; no bump sound when hitting wall on ice (HitWall suppressed)
Implement ledge jump: tiles $A0-$A7 encode which directions allow jumping via low 3 bits mask table; plays SFX_JUMP_OVER_LEDGE on success; movement result = PLAYERMOVEMENT_JUMP

source_data.overworld_engine.md:
## Player Movement (player_movement.asm)

### DoPlayerMovement
1. GetDPad -- Read joypad, apply downhill forcing
2. TranslateIntoMovement -- Based on player state:

Player States:
| PLAYER_NORMAL | Standard walking |
| PLAYER_BIKE | Fast movement (STEP_BIKE) |
| PLAYER_SURF | Water movement |
| PLAYER_SKATE | Ice sliding |

Movement Pipeline (Normal/Bike):
1. CheckForced -- On ice, forces same direction
2. GetAction -- Maps D-pad to walking direction, facing, delta X/Y, collision tile
3. CheckTile -- Forced movement tiles override input
4. CheckTurning -- If facing != walking direction, turn first
5. TryStep -- Check land permissions, NPC collision, ice, bike speed
6. TryJump -- Ledge hopping (HI_NYBBLE_LEDGES tiles)
7. CheckWarp -- Edge warp detection

Step Types:
| STEP_WALK | Normal (slow_step) |
| STEP_BIKE | Fast (big_step) |
| STEP_ICE | Slide (fast_slide_step) |
| STEP_LEDGE | Jump (jump_step) |
| STEP_TURN | Turn in place |

Movement Results:
- PLAYERMOVEMENT_NORMAL (0) -- No movement
- PLAYERMOVEMENT_WARP (1) -- Entered warp
- PLAYERMOVEMENT_TURN (2) -- Changed facing
- PLAYERMOVEMENT_FINISH (4) -- Step started
- PLAYERMOVEMENT_JUMP (7) -- Ledge hop

### Ledge System
Collision tiles $A0-$A7 (HI_NYBBLE_LEDGES). Each tile encodes which facing directions allow jumping. Plays SFX_JUMP_OVER_LEDGE. The low 3 bits index into a mask table.

### Collision Checks
- CheckLandPerms: Tile permissions (wTilePermissions) AND facing direction
- CheckNPC: IsNPCAtCoord checks all 16 object structs
- CheckStrengthBoulder: If Strength active and STRENGTH_BOULDER_F, set BOULDER_MOVING_F

### Ice Sliding
- Input locked to current direction (CheckForced)
- Movement continues until hitting wall or non-ice tile
- If hitting wall on ice, no bump sound (HitWall)
source_data.collision_and_tilesets.md:
## Collision Tile Types
| LAND_TILE | walkable | Player can walk on this tile |
| WATER_TILE | surfable | Requires Surf to traverse |
| WALL_TILE | impassable | Player cannot walk through |

### Floor/Walkable (LAND_TILE)
| 0x23 | COLL_ICE | Ice floor (player slides) |
| 0x40 | COLL_BRAKE | Stops sliding on ice |
| 0x41-0x44 | COLL_WALK_RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN | Forced movement tiles |
| 0x60 | COLL_PIT | Fall-through pit |

### Warp Tiles
| 0x70 | COLL_WARP_CARPET_DOWN | Warp with downward animation |
| 0x71 | COLL_DOOR | Standard door warp |
| 0x72 | COLL_LADDER | Ladder warp |
| 0x73 | COLL_STAIRCASE_73 | Staircase warp |
| 0x74 | COLL_CAVE_74 | Cave entrance warp |

unstructured_notes: Player movement is the most frame-sensitive system in the overworld. The turning delay (CheckTurning) is important for authentic feel — the player must face a direction before walking into it. PLAYER_BIKE moves at big_step speed which is faster than normal walking but can still be blocked by walls and ledges. The 8 ledge tile variants ($A0-$A7) each correspond to a directional bitmask — $A0 might be 'south only' while $A4 might be 'south and east'. The downhill forcing in GetDPad creates the effect of being pushed downhill on specific map tiles. NPC collision checks all 16 object slots which includes both NPCs and interactable objects.
